stories.title: Jewish High School Championed by Jeff Sutton Secures $30M in Construction Financing
stories.excerpt: A Jewish school in Brooklyn’s Gravesend neighborhood has received approval from the state attorney general for a $30 million construction loan, Commercial Observer has learned. Magen David Yeshivah, which operates multiple day schools in Gravesend, secured the $30 million loan from BankUnited for the continued construction of a new Sephardic high school along McDonald Avenue. The floating-rate, three-year loan agreement mortgages Magen David’s nearby elementary school property at 2130 McDonald Avenue. Religious corporations in New York must have approval from the state attorney general or a supreme court justice before mortgaging, selling or leasing most real estate. The school acquired its future home across three sites — 1905, 1911 and 1915 McDonald Avenue — in 2023 for $13.5 million. Jeff Sutton, a prominent retail landlord and an active Magen David trustee, assisted the school in the deal, The Real Deal reported at the time. It is unclear whether Sutton, or any brokers, lent their expertise in arranging the new loan terms. A diagram of a planned school at 370 Quentin Road, Brooklyn. DIAGRAM: Sherida Paulsen Architect via NYC Department of Buildings Plans for the $50 million high school span 81,000 square feet, PincusCo first reported. Remaining construction costs total $37.46 million, and the building is slated for completion in early 2027. Magen David school, founded in 1946, sold its former high school at 7801 Bay Parkway to Success Academy Charter Schools for $37.2 million in 2024, according to TRD. It continues to operate an early childhood center at 2170 McDonald Avenue and its nearby elementary school. Spokespeople for Magen David and BankUnited did not immediately respond to requests for comment. Emily Davis can be reached at edavis@commercialobserver.com.
bullets: Magen David Yeshivah secures $30M construction loan from BankUnited for Brooklyn high school.
School acquired three McDonald Ave sites in 2023 for $13.5M; floating-rate, 3-year term.
Retail landlord Jeff Sutton assisted in original land acquisition; minimal relevance to multifamily.
stories.summary: A Brooklyn-based Jewish high school has secured a $30 million floating-rate construction loan from BankUnited to continue building a new Sephardic high school in Gravesend. The school had acquired three adjacent properties on McDonald Avenue in 2023 for $13.5 million, with retail investor Jeff Sutton playing a role in the original transaction. The loan is structured over three years and is secured by the school's nearby elementary school property.
stories.impact: Minimal direct impact to multifamily development. This is a nonprofit education capital project in Brooklyn with no supply, demand, or financing implications for apartment development.

stories.title: L.A. County Property Values Hit $2.27 Trillion Despite Wildfires, Sluggish Market
stories.excerpt: Los Angeles County’s taxable property values reached a record $2.272 trillion at the start of 2026, rising 4.42 percent year-over-year despite the devastating wildfires 17 months ago, and despite a real estate market that in some ways is still recovering from the COVID pandemic. The assessment roll reflects property values as of Jan. 1, and it helps government officials prepare annual budgets and forecast tax revenue. The latest assessment roll includes nearly 2.4 million taxable parcels. L.A. County Assessor Jeff Prang announced that the assessment roll grew by $96 billion from 2025, making it the 16th straight year of value growth. The roll is expected to generate more than $27 billion in property taxes for public schools, health care, public safety and other government services. “When I presented the forecast to the Board of Supervisors in May, I emphasized 2025-2026 was a challenging year, and the impact of [last] January’s devastating wildfires will be felt for years to come,” Prang said in a statement. “Even so, Los Angeles County’s economy continues to demonstrate remarkable resilience.” Property transfers led the way by adding $49 billion in assessed value, while new construction contributed more than $12 billion. The maximum 2 percent inflation adjustment permitted under state law added another $43 billion. L.A. County home sales moderated during 2025, but the median home sale price still reached $982,000. The city of Los Angeles posted the highest assessed value at $926.1 billion. Long Beach hit $84.5 billion, and Santa Monica reached $54.9 billion. Irwindale’s assessed value jumped 15 percent for the most gain over the year, followed by Vernon at 13.3 percent, and Hidden Hills at 11.7 percent. Gregory Cornfield can be reached at gcornfield@commercialobserver.com.
bullets: L.A. County taxable property values reach $2.272T as of Jan. 1, 2026; +4.42% YoY growth.
Assessment reflects 16th consecutive year of value growth despite January 2025 wildfires.
Property tax revenue forecast exceeds $27B for public services; 2.4M taxable parcels assessed.
stories.summary: Los Angeles County's assessed property values hit a record $2.272 trillion as of January 1, 2026, growing 4.42 percent year-over-year despite devastating January 2025 wildfires and a sluggish post-pandemic real estate market. The assessment roll added $96 billion in value from 2025 and marks the 16th consecutive year of growth. The valuation supports a projected $27 billion in property tax revenue for schools, health, and public safety.
stories.impact: Sustained property value appreciation in L.A. County signals resilience in underlying real estate fundamentals and tax base stability, which supports municipal budgets and public infrastructure investment—indirect tailwinds for multifamily demand in Los Angeles. However, higher assessed values may increase property tax burdens on rental properties and reduce development margins; developers should monitor Prop 13 reassessment timing and reassessment of completed projects.

stories.title: Boeing Adding to Mesa Manufacturing Facility
stories.excerpt: A Mesa planning board will consider a nearly 268,000 proposed expansion to an existing Boeing manufacturing facility on the east side of Greenfield Road north of McDowell Road. (Photo courtesy of Todd Photographic Services) AZBEX reports the one-story building is proposed as a Boeing Fabrication Center, adjacent to an existing Boeing Fabrication Center totaling 158,000 square feet, and will be used “for fabrication of highly specialized carbon military parts. (Photo courtesy of Todd Photographic Services) Boeing’s Arizona presence is anchored in Mesa, where its campus has served for decades as the home of AH-64 Apache helicopter production and advanced aerospace manufacturing. Boeing employs more than 4,500 Arizonans, works with more than 270 suppliers and vendors statewide, supports an estimated 49,000 direct and indirect jobs and generated $1.1 billion in supplier spending in 2025. Beyond Mesa, Boeing’s Arizona operations extend to Chandler and Goodyear, supporting a broad aerospace value chain, including manufacturing, logistics, supply chain management and engineering. Connect Texas Multifamily in Dallas on August 13 brings together the owners, developers, brokers, investors, and lenders shaping the future of Texas multifamily for a day of market intelligence, networking, and actionable insights. Join 300+ decision-makers and be part of the conversations driving the Texas multifamily market. Register today: www.ConnectTXMF2026.com. The post Boeing Adding to Mesa Manufacturing Facility appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: Boeing expands Mesa, Arizona manufacturing with 268K-sq-ft Fabrication Center for military carbon parts.
Boeing Arizona operations employ 4,500+ workers, support 49K direct/indirect jobs, $1.1B supplier spend.
Expansion signals aerospace sector strength and sustained high-wage employment in Phoenix metro.
stories.summary: Boeing is adding a 268,000-square-foot Fabrication Center adjacent to an existing 158,000-sq-ft facility in Mesa, Arizona, to produce specialized carbon military parts. The company operates a major Arizona footprint spanning Mesa, Chandler, and Goodyear, with 4,500+ direct employees, 270+ suppliers, and an estimated $1.1 billion in supplier spending in 2025. The expansion underscores Boeing's anchoring role in Arizona aerospace manufacturing.
stories.impact: Boeing's Mesa expansion signals sustained high-wage employment and aerospace sector demand in the Phoenix metro—strong demand tailwind for multifamily in Mesa, Chandler, and surrounding Scottsdale/Tempe submarkets. Aerospace engineers and specialized manufacturing workers command premium salaries; developers should target Class A rentals near Boeing facilities and explore build-to-suit opportunities for workforce housing in east Phoenix corridor.

stories.title: Thorofare Capital CEO Kevin Miller Exits Company After 17 Years
stories.excerpt: One year after losing its three head originators, Thorofare Capital’s shakeup is now evident at the very top of the C-suite level. Kevin Miller, who spent the last nearly 17 years as Thorofare’s CEO, is exiting the L.A.-based lender, according to sources familiar with the departure and an internal memo shared with Commercial Observer. “We thank Kevin for founding the firm and for his long-tenured service and role in the firm’s growth and success,” the July 15 memo sent to Thorofare limited partners said. The circumstances surrounding Miller’s departure were not immediately known, nor was the timetable for naming a replacement. Miller did not immediately return requests for comment. The loss of Miller comes on the heels of another big leadership hit Thorofare took last year culminating with the company parting ways with Felix Gutnikov, h​​ead of originations, in July 2025. Brendan Miller, Thorofare’s president and chief investment officer, told CO at the time the move was part of the company’s strategy to head in a “new direction” toward a “regional-focused origination model.” Gutnikov was let go by Thorofare soon after David Perlman decided to leave his role leading the firm’s New York City office in early 2025 to relocate to Auckland, New Zealand. Thorofare also saw Jonathan Hart, head of the lender’s Dallas office , leave for S3 Capital in June 2025. It seems that Brendan Miller will now lead the company in Kevin Miller’s absence. “The management team led by Brendan Miller and the broader organization remain well positioned to continue executing the fund’s investment strategy and to serve its investors, maintaining the disciplined approach that has guided the firm throughout its 16-year history,” the July memo stated. “We appreciate your continued confidence and partnership and are very excited about the future of the firm.” Brendan Miller did not immediately return a request for comment. Thorofare saw large-sale growth under Kevin Miller’s leadership,
bullets: Thorofare Capital CEO Kevin Miller exits after 17 years; firm lost three head originators in 2025.
Leadership turnover signals internal distress at L.A.-based construction lender amid market consolidation.
Capital availability uncertainty for multifamily debt; CMBS and agency lenders fill origination void.
stories.summary: Kevin Miller, founder and CEO of Los Angeles-based multifamily lender Thorofare Capital for 17 years, has exited the firm, marking the second major leadership departure in less than a year. The company previously lost its head of originations, Felix Gutnikov, in July 2025 as part of a strategic pivot toward "regional-focused" lending. The reasons for Miller's departure were not disclosed, nor was a timeline for a successor announced.
stories.impact: Thorofare Capital's leadership exodus signals stress in the nonbank multifamily lending sector and consolidation pressure; originators and PE debt funds are capturing deal flow. Multifamily developers should expect tighter underwriting, higher leverage spreads, and reduced floating-rate capacity from regional lenders. Developers should diversify funding sources toward Fannie Mae, Freddie Mac, CMBS pools, and debt-focused PE funds to maintain flexibility and pricing.

stories.title: StreetLights Advancing Apartment Venture at Former Plano Office Campus
stories.excerpt: StreetLights Residential has secured $54 million in equity, along with $111.5 million in construction loans, for a new 22-story Plano apartment complex expected to open in 2029. The project will be part of the conversion of The Park of Legacy, a 107-acre office campus into a mixed-use development with apartments, hotels and restaurants. It used to be JCPenney’s headquarters. Northmarq’s Dallas debt and equity team — Joel Heikenfeld, Charlie Rimkus and Charlie Robinson — arranged the equity financing on behalf of StreetLights. The transaction is structured on a five-year term. Residence sizes will range from 682 to 2,843 square feet. The project will also build three 3-bedroom townhomes ranging from 3,283 to 3,316 square feet next to the high-rise. Amenities will include a pool with private cabanas, a sky lounge with panoramic views, a golf simulator, a fitness center and coworking spaces. Residents will also have access to a library and will have pet-friendly features. Connect Texas Multifamily in Dallas on August 13 brings together the owners, developers, brokers, investors, and lenders shaping the future of Texas multifamily for a day of market intelligence, networking, and actionable insights. Join 300+ decision-makers and be part of the conversations driving the Texas multifamily market. Register today: www.ConnectTXMF2026.com. The post StreetLights Advancing Apartment Venture at Former Plano Office Campus appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: StreetLights Residential secures $54M equity + $111.5M construction debt for 22-story Plano tower.
Project converts former JCPenney HQ office campus (107 acres) into mixed-use with hotels, retail.
Expected opening 2029; market demand for office-to-residential conversion in Dallas-Fort Worth expanding.
stories.summary: StreetLights Residential has secured $54 million in equity and $111.5 million in construction financing for a 22-story luxury apartment tower in Plano, Texas, expected to open in 2029. The project is part of a broader redevelopment of The Park at Legacy, a 107-acre former JCPenney headquarters office campus being converted into mixed-use with apartments, hotels, and restaurants. Unit sizes range from 682 to 2,843 sq ft; amenities include sky lounge, golf simulator, and coworking spaces.
stories.impact: Office-to-multifamily conversion in Plano signals strong rental demand and developer confidence in Dallas-Fort Worth submarket fundamentals. Legacy office parks are repositioning as mixed-use anchors; available capital for construction debt ($111.5M) indicates lender appetite for trophy conversion deals. Developers should identify secondary office parks in Dallas-Fort Worth suburbs (Plano, Frisco, Lewisville) as acquisition targets, as obsolete office space commands lower basis and conversion economics improve with residential rent premiums.

stories.title: Podcast | How AI Helps Brokers Prospect Smarter, with DealGround CEO Dan Mosher
stories.excerpt: Dan Mosher is the CEO of DealGround, an AI-native platform helping commercial real estate brokers and investors find deals faster by organizing property data, identifying opportunities, and connecting directly with property owners. A veteran Silicon Valley entrepreneur, operator, and investor, Dan previously built and led the Merchant team at Postmates through its acquisition by Uber, served as President of Presto, and helped scale BrightRoll tenfold before its acquisition by Yahoo. Live from ICSC+Proptech in Las Vegas. (01:09) AI Survey with FirstAmerican (03:07) How DealGround Helps Brokers Find More Deals (06:08) Why AI Adoption Outpaces Trust (10:14) MCPs, Integrations & Fitting Into Broker Workflows Learn more: –Visit DealGround –Dan Mosher on LinkedIn –Study: First American Data & Analytics & DealGround on AI Adoption in CRE –ICSC+Proptech NYC Real Estate Week: -CTO Exchange & Hackathon: 3-in-1 event on Oct. 19 featuring C-suite roundtables, a hackathon and the Power AI & Tech Awards reception (invite-only). Email ecohen@commercialobserver.com for info. Connect with Tangent: -Edward Cohen on LinkedIn -Zach Aarons on LinkedIn -Tangent on LinkedIn CRE Events coming up: –Institutional Investors & PE Forum: in NYC on 9/16 –South Florida Capital & Development Forum: in Miami on 10/15 –All upcoming events Disclaimer: Commercial Observer Tangent podcast is for entertainment and informational purposes only, you should not construe any such information or other material as legal, tax, investment, financial, or other advice. Nothing contained on our program constitutes a solicitation, recommendation, endorsement, or offer by Tangent, Commercial Observer, MetaProp, or any third party guest to buy or sell any securities, public or private, other financial instruments or funds.
bullets: DealGround AI platform helps CRE brokers identify and connect with deal targets faster.
Platform addresses broker workflow integration and data organization; adoption outpaces trust.
Minimal relevance to multifamily development underwriting, financing, or site acquisition.
stories.summary: DealGround, an AI-native platform founded by Silicon Valley veteran Dan Mosher, helps commercial real estate brokers and investors identify and pursue deal opportunities by organizing property data, identifying prospects, and connecting directly with owners. The platform streamlines broker workflows and aims to reduce friction in deal sourcing. A concurrent First American study found that AI adoption in CRE outpaces broker and investor trust in its outputs.
stories.impact: Minimal direct impact to multifamily development strategy. AI-powered deal sourcing tools will marginally accelerate land acquisition timelines and improve competitive bid positioning, but no material effect on capital, supply, or demand fundamentals.

stories.title: Private Equity Turns to Debt Investments to Add Multifamily Assets
stories.excerpt: U.S. multifamily advertised rents increased by an average of $4 in June to $1,763, continuing the recent trend of moderate growth, Yardi Matrix reported. Rents rose 0.7% during the second quarter of 2026 and 1.0% in the first half of the year. Both metrics fall well below the pace seen in both the immediate pre-pandemic period and the first few post-pandemic years. However, Yardi Matrix reported, slow rent growth has not stopped investors from allotting capital to the sector. At the same time, growth in property sales has been weak, leading private equity to book multifamily through debt investments at differing levels of the capital stack. “Debt funds are grabbing a growing share of the pie, agency lenders Fannie Mae and Freddie Mac have expanded their securitization offerings, and pooled multifamily-only CMBS deals are making a comeback for the first time since the 2008 financial crisis,” reported Yardi Matrix. Stay ahead of the multifamily market at Connect Apartments. Gain firsthand insights from industry leaders on capital markets, development, operations, financing, and investment trends while expanding your network with the people making deals happen. See you on September 22! The post Private Equity Turns to Debt Investments to Add Multifamily Assets appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: U.S. multifamily rents +$4 to $1,763/mo in June 2026; +0.7% Q2, +1.0% H1 YoY.
Rent growth well below pre-pandemic and early post-pandemic pace; weak property sales persist.
PE debt funds and CMBS pools expanding, capturing share from traditional lenders and property sales.
stories.summary: U.S. multifamily advertised rents increased by just $4 to $1,763 in June 2026, with second-quarter growth of 0.7 percent and first-half-year growth of 1.0 percent—well below pre-pandemic and immediate post-pandemic trends. Despite sluggish rent growth, investor capital remains committed to the sector, but weak property sales have prompted private equity and debt funds to pursue multifamily exposure through debt investments across the capital stack. Agency lenders Fannie Mae and Freddie Mac have expanded securitization offerings, and multifamily-only CMBS deals are resurfacing for the first time since 2008.
stories.impact: Muted rent growth signals supply-demand equilibrium or slight oversupply in many markets; new development must justify underwriting on conservative rent growth assumptions (0.7–1.0% annually). Shift toward debt-focused PE entry and CMBS resurgence indicates strong financing competition and downward spread pressure on construction debt. Developers should expect cap rates to compress and leverage ratios to tighten as floating-rate debt becomes pricier; consider fixed-rate locks on development loans.

stories.title: Signorelli Reveals Two New Phases for New Caney’s Valley Ranch
stories.excerpt: The Signorelli Company announced Commerce District at Valley Ranch, a mixed-use destination that represents the next phase in the evolution of the 1,400-acre Valley Ranch master-planned community in New Caney, Texas. Situated on 55 acres, Commerce District will encompass 400,000 square feet of commercial development and a 600-unit luxury multifamily community. Developed in two phases, district construction is scheduled to begin in late July 2026. The first phase, Commerce West, will be anchored by a new 120,000-square-foot H-E-B supermarket. With an anticipated opening in Fall, 2027, this location is H-E-B’s first store in New Caney. In addition to H-E-B, Commerce West includes approximately 19,500 square feet of architecturally distinctive retail space and two premier outparcel opportunities for complementary restaurants, retail and service-oriented businesses. Commerce East, the future second phase, will feature retail, restaurants, multifamily residences, and Class A office space. The post Signorelli Reveals Two New Phases for New Caney’s Valley Ranch appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: Signorelli Company launches Commerce District at Valley Ranch in New Caney, Texas: 600 units + 400K SF retail.
Phase 1 anchored by H-E-B supermarket (first in New Caney); construction begins late July 2026.
Master-planned community (1,400 acres total) expanding; retail anchors support residential densification.
stories.summary: The Signorelli Company has announced Commerce District at Valley Ranch, a mixed-use development within the 1,400-acre Valley Ranch master-planned community in New Caney, Texas. Phase 1 (Commerce West) will include a 120,000-sq-ft H-E-B supermarket—the chain's first in New Caney—plus 19,500 sq ft of retail and outparcels. Phase 2 (Commerce East) will feature retail, restaurants, multifamily residences, and Class A office. The full district encompasses 600 luxury units across 55 acres; construction begins late July 2026 with H-E-B opening in Fall 2027.
stories.impact: Master-planned community expansion with major grocery anchor signals population influx and demand for suburban workforce housing in New Caney/Houston exurbs. H-E-B entry validates market size and consumer purchasing power; grocery anchors drive multifamily absorption by attracting retail-dependent demographics. Developers should monitor Valley Ranch for adjacent land opportunities and consider similar retail-anchored multifamily pods in outer Houston and DFW rings.

stories.title: Restaurateur James O’Brien Bringing Wine Bar to Naftali’s Williamsburg Wharf
stories.excerpt: Some wine and a walk by the Williamsburg waterfront? Sounds like the perfect evening. James O’Brien, the restaurateur behind Cobble Hill’s popular Italian restaurant Popina, has signed a 2,000-square-foot lease at Naftali Group’s Williamsburg Wharf to open a new wine bar, the developer announced Tuesday. The yet-to-be-named wine bar will open at 484 Kent Avenue in 2027. The length of the lease and the asking rent were not disclosed, but the average asking rent for retail space in Williamsburg north of Sixth Street was $400 per square foot, according to the Real Estate Board of New York’s 2025 Brooklyn retail report. “James has earned a reputation for creating restaurants that feel deeply rooted in their communities, and we’re thrilled to partner with him on an entirely new concept for Williamsburg Wharf,” Miki Naftali, chairman and CEO of Naftali, said in a statement. “Popina has built a genuine following in Brooklyn, and we know what he will introduce will further strengthen the sense of community and authenticity that defines the destination we’ve sought to curate along the Williamsburg waterfront.” Restaurant industry advisory firm Friend of Chef represented both Naftali and O’Brien in the deal. The names of the brokers involved were not immediately available. A spokesperson for Friend of Chef did not immediately respond to Commercial Observer’s request for comment. Williamsburg Wharf is a 3.75-acre resort-style waterfront residential development featuring 950 residential units — both condominium and rental units, a portion of which are designated as affordable housing. The first phase of the project was completed last year, and the project is set to top out sometime in 2028. But there’s already an abundance of retail tenants along the Williamsburg Wharf stretch, including the Goods Mart, Breads Bakery and Naked Tomato, a new restaurant slated to open later this year. “I’m really excited to be part of what’s happeni
bullets: James O'Brien (Popina restaurateur) leases 2,000 SF wine bar at Naftali Group's Williamsburg Wharf.
Asking rent ~$400/SF for Williamsburg retail north of Sixth Street; opening 2027 at 484 Kent Ave.
Retail tenant quality signals leasing momentum in waterfront mixed-use; residential demand implied.
stories.summary: James O'Brien, the restaurateur behind popular Italian restaurant Popina in Cobble Hill, has signed a 2,000-square-foot lease for a new wine bar at Naftali Group's Williamsburg Wharf at 484 Kent Avenue, opening in 2027. The average asking rent for Williamsburg retail north of Sixth Street is approximately $400 per square foot. O'Brien's brand and restaurant success signal developer confidence in the Williamsburg waterfront mixed-use tenant base.
stories.impact: Retail tenant quality and brand signaling at Williamsburg Wharf validates residential anchor demand in the submarket. Strong independent restaurant operators (vs. chains) indicate walkable, affluent demographic and pricing power. Developers should view Williamsburg waterfront retail leasing as a proxy for adjacent residential rental demand; pre-leasing critical retail tenants (grocery, dining, fitness) locks in residential NOI assumptions and supports construction financing appetite.

stories.title: JLL Inks Heritage Plaza Leasing Deal
stories.excerpt: JLL has recently been selected as the exclusive leasing team for Heritage Plaza. The 1.15 million-square-foot, 51-story property is located at 1111 Bagby Street JLL’s Jon Dutton, Tyler Garrett, and Paul Frazier will lead leasing efforts on behalf of Brookfield Properties and AEW Capital Management. Heritage Plaza is distinguished by its signature architecture and prominent position in Downtown Houston. The tower offers a thoughtfully modernized workplace experience, highlighted by expansive lobby renovations, hospitality-inspired amenities, premier connectivity and immediate access to the city’s top cultural, dining and outdoor destinations. According to JLL’s Q1 2026 Houston Office Market Report, tenant demand remains concentrated among Tier 1 office assets as the flight-to-quality trend continues to shape leasing activity. JLL’s assignment at Heritage Plaza underscores Downtown Houston’s ongoing momentum and the continued appeal of well-located, amenity-rich office environments that enhance the employee experience while supporting connectivity and long-term business objectives. The post JLL Inks Heritage Plaza Leasing Deal appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: JLL selected as exclusive leaser for Heritage Plaza, 1.15M SF, 51-story office tower in Downtown Houston.
Brookfield Properties and AEW Capital Management operate asset; flight-to-quality trend favors Tier 1 office.
Downtown Houston office momentum driven by amenity-rich, well-connected Class A assets.
stories.summary: JLL has been selected as exclusive leasing agent for Heritage Plaza, a 1.15 million-square-foot, 51-story office tower at 1111 Bagby Street in Downtown Houston, on behalf of owners Brookfield Properties and AEW Capital Management. The tower features a modernized lobby, hospitality-driven amenities, and connectivity to Houston's cultural and dining districts. JLL's Q1 2026 Houston Office Market Report notes that tenant demand is concentrated in Tier 1 assets as flight-to-quality continues.
stories.impact: Minimal impact to multifamily development. Office leasing activity in Downtown Houston does not directly drive apartment supply or demand, though successful office retenancy may indirectly support downtown mixed-use and residential conversion projects by stabilizing the district.

stories.title: PTM Partners, Peacock launch subsidy-free workforce housing platform
stories.excerpt: Florida-based PTM Partners and Peacock Capital launched Inception Housing, a platform designed to deliver lower-cost housing without relying on government aid.  The venture targets middle-income renters earning between 80 and 120 percent of the area median income, cutting development costs by handling much of the design, construction and material procurement in-house, according to a news release.  By using a standardized building design and cold-formed steel purchased directly from suppliers, Inception Housing can cut hard construction costs by about 25 percent compared to similar products, PTM Partners CEO Michael Tillman said. The platform’s strategy delivers buildings in six to seven months, […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: PTM Partners + Peacock Capital launch Inception Housing platform for subsidy-free workforce housing.
Cold-formed steel + standardized design cuts hard costs 25%, builds in 6–7 months vs. 12–14 months.
Targets 80–120% AMI renters; South Florida pilot (Magnolia Flats, 88 units Orlando); state shortage 120K units.
stories.summary: Florida-based PTM Partners and Peacock Capital have launched Inception Housing, a platform delivering lower-cost multifamily without government subsidies by using standardized design, cold-formed steel procurement, and in-house construction. The model cuts hard construction costs by approximately 25 percent and reduces delivery time to six to seven months—a 50 percent improvement over traditional stick-built construction. The platform targets middle-income renters earning 80–120 percent of area median income. Inception has already launched Magnolia Flats near Orlando (88 units across existing townhomes and new buildings), and South Florida faces a shortage of 30,460+ units statewide.
stories.impact: Inception Housing's cost and timeline arbitrage model signals a structural shift toward efficiency-driven, subsidy-free workforce housing delivery in supply-constrained markets. 25 percent hard-cost reduction and 50 percent faster delivery enable smaller margins and lower rents while maintaining feasibility—direct competitive pressure on traditional builders. Developers should evaluate cold-formed steel and design standardization for Florida workforce projects; model may spread to other high-cost, supply-short metros (Austin, Nashville, Denver). Florida's Live Local Act tax incentives amplify feasibility; similar state programs create tailwinds for modular/standardized product.

stories.title: Dallas apartment pipeline gets a boost with pair of financing deals
stories.excerpt: Two developers of a pair of apartment complexes in the Dallas-Fort Worth metroplex recently secured financing for their separate projects.  Endeavor Real Estate locked in financing for their luxury high-rise at 2700 McKinney Avenue named Lucille. Additionally, StreetLights Residential snagged $54 million in equity financing for an apartment tower in Plano, Texas, which will be built in the redevelopment of The Park at Legacy at 6501 Legacy Drive.  Lucille will feature 265 units across 22 stories in Uptown, according to the Dallas Morning News. The development is helmed by Austin-based Endeavor and equity partner Canyon Partners Real Estate. Construction on […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Endeavor Real Estate + Canyon Partners secure financing for Lucille, 265-unit luxury tower in Dallas Uptown.
StreetLights secures $54M equity for Plano office-to-residential conversion (22-story, 2029 opening).
Dallas-Fort Worth multifamily pipeline accelerating; Northmarq and other lenders active in dual transactions.
stories.summary: Two Dallas-area multifamily projects recently secured financing: Endeavor Real Estate and Canyon Partners' Lucille, a 265-unit, 22-story luxury high-rise in Uptown (2700 McKinney Avenue, opening late 2028), and StreetLights Residential's 22-story tower in Plano (opening 2029, part of The Park at Legacy office conversion). Lucille spans 320,000 square feet with 10,000 sq ft ground-floor retail, rooftop pool, observation deck, and below-grade parking. Both projects are backed by strong institutional partners and construction lending.
stories.impact: Dual Dallas-Fort Worth multifamily closings signal strong capital formation and lender confidence in metropolitan rental fundamentals. Uptown financial district growth (Lucille) and suburban office-to-residential conversions (Plano) point to dispersed demand across submarket tiers. Developers should prioritize Uptown infill and secondary office parks in Plano/Frisco as acquisition targets; construction financing availability remains robust for high-quality sponsor/JV partnerships.

stories.title: Texas’ top construction permits for the week ending July 14, 2026
stories.excerpt: New Construction Fort Worth’s public hospital John Peter Smith Hospital filed the state’s biggest construction project of the week to build a $300 million central utility plant at 1575 South Main Street on Fort Worth’s South Side. Construction on the 113,000-square-foot facility started at the end of June and is expected to wrap up in May 2028.  Job filing number: TABS2026024842 Houston Independent School District filed plans to build a 216,000-square-foot career and technical center at 9220 Jutland Road in Houston. Construction on the project, which will feature classrooms, labs, a multipurpose center, kitchen and administration spaces, is expected to […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Fort Worth John Peter Smith Hospital: $300M central utility plant (113K SF), June 2026–May 2028.
Houston ISD: $216K SF career/technical center at $9220 Jutland Rd, March 2027–Sept 2028.
Amazon: $98M distribution center near Dallas (Terrell), 1.2M SF, August 2026–August 2027.
stories.summary: Texas's top construction permits in the week of July 14, 2026 reflect large institutional and logistics projects: Fort Worth's John Peter Smith Hospital filed a $300 million, 113,000-sq-ft central utility plant (June 2026–May 2028); Houston ISD filed a 216,000-sq-ft career and technical center ($9220 Jutland Road, March 2027–Sept 2028); Amazon filed a $98 million, 1.2 million-sq-ft distribution center in Terrell, near Dallas (August 2026–August 2027). Additional permits include Dallas Museum of Art ($20M mechanical/fire upgrades) and a $14 million tech tenant improvement in Austin (Shoal Creek Walk office).
stories.impact: Amazon's Terrell distribution center signals ongoing e-commerce logistics growth and population concentration in Dallas-Fort Worth's outer ring—demand driver for workforce housing near distribution hubs. Hospital and school projects indicate public sector capital deployment and population growth expectations. Healthcare expansion (John Peter Smith) may drive demand for affordable multifamily near employment clusters in South Dallas and Fort Worth. Developers should identify land near Terrell, Weatherford, and South Fort Worth for workforce/value-add multifamily to serve Amazon and logistics workforce.

stories.title: Dolphins out, superyachts in: David Martin’s marina plan at former Seaquarium getting underway
stories.excerpt: The site of the former Miami Seaquarium on Virginia Key is headed for a major redevelopment.  Miami-Dade County and Terra Group’s David Martin want to trade dolphin shows for superyachts with an application to build what could be the largest marina in the county, the Miami Herald reported.  The application, submitted to state regulators, calls for 325 boat slips, about 90 of which could accommodate yachts longer than 80 feet, and a 500-boat dry stack, the outlet said. The plan includes a fuel depot, a network of floating docks that extends halfway across the channel separating Virginia Key from nearby […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Terra Group/David Martin plans 325-slip marina at former Miami Seaquarium (Virginia Key); 90 slips for 80+ ft yachts.
Miami-Dade negotiating ground lease; $19K SF fuel depot, dry stack (500 boats), wave break/fishing pier.
FDEP regulators flagged wetlands/water quality concerns; final lease terms pending environmental approvals.
stories.summary: Terra Group CEO David Martin has applied to convert the former Miami Seaquarium site (4400 Rickenbacker Causeway) into a 325-slip marina with dry stack capacity for 500 boats, anchored by 90 slips accommodating yachts longer than 80 feet. The plan includes a 19,000-sq-ft fuel depot, floating docks, wave break/fishing pier, and fuel docks. Miami-Dade owns the land and is negotiating a ground lease with Martin (who receives 95 percent of slip profits). The Florida Department of Environmental Protection has raised concerns about dredging, wetland impacts, water quality, and sewage/fuel management; final approvals pending.
stories.impact: Minor indirect impact to Miami multifamily. While the marina redevelopment will draw visitors and premium waterfront spending, it does not materially affect residential supply, construction costs, or demand-side economics. Successful approvals may signal continued Miami-Dade waterfront development momentum, indirectly benefiting nearby mixed-use residential projects (e.g., Brickell, Wynwood), but primary implication is leisure/hospitality, not multifamily fundamentals.

stories.title: TIF funding for the 78 gets key approval for $425M for needed infrastructure
stories.excerpt: The Chicago City Council Committee on Finance approved a deal to fund $425 million of infrastructure at the 78, a mixed-use development that will include a privately-funded new stadium for the Major League Soccer franchise the Chicago Fire.  The deal would repay developer Related Midwest for project-supporting structures using tax increment financing money, including $287 million in TIF money from a nearby district. The next step in the deal will be getting the formal green light from the City Council, who are set to hold a final vote on the agreement on Wednesday, according to Crain’s.  Joe Mansueto, the Fire’s […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Chicago City Council Finance Committee approves $425M TIF funding for The 78 mixed-use development.
Related Midwest to receive $287M in TIF redirects from Canal/Congress district to fund infrastructure.
MLS stadium (privately funded) plus broad mixed-use (retail, office, residential); final City Council vote set.
stories.summary: The Chicago City Council Finance Committee has approved a $425 million tax increment financing (TIF) package to fund infrastructure at The 78, a mixed-use development by Related Midwest that includes a privately-funded Chicago Fire MLS stadium. The city will redirect $287 million in TIF proceeds from the Canal/Congress TIF district into the Roosevelt/Clark TIF district to reimburse Related for project-supporting infrastructure (roads, utilities). A final City Council vote is scheduled for Wednesday. Alderman Bill Conway raised concerns that the transfer may drain TIF resources from Union Station and public transit projects.
stories.impact: TIF reallocation to The 78 may constrain TIF availability for other Chicago mixed-use and transit-oriented residential projects. However, successful infrastructure funding at The 78 creates a proof-of-concept for TIF-backed development financing in Chicago and may improve appetite for future downtown multifamily projects with strong public-private partnerships. Developers should monitor final City Council vote; if approved, it may open TIF tooling for other downtown residential conversion projects but will tighten public financing competition for suburban and mid-market developments.

stories.title: Housing Notes: Developers are in on the joke: Apologize later
stories.excerpt: I have long had a fascination with the grit it takes to be a Manhattan developer. After all, I taught market analysis to a class of approximately 150 graduate students in Columbia’s Master of Science in Real Estate Development (MSRED) program for 8 years. My students have ended up all over the U.S. and hopefully they learned not to accept “no” as an answer when they’re out in the world. That’s the dividing line since developers tend to ignore the word “no” and keep pushing, when non-developers like me would cower in fear and self-doubt. It helps to look at […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Harry Macklowe (Manhattan developer legend) conducted midnight demolitions; developers ignore 'no' systematically.
Nathan Berman/MetroLoft converting former Pfizer HQ to residential; partial collapse raises regulatory/execution risk.
Essay on developer psychology, risk appetite, and survival; minimal actionable market intelligence.
stories.summary: A personal essay reflecting on Manhattan developer culture and risk-taking, centering on legendary developer Harry Macklowe's willingness to pursue aggressive strategies (midnight demolitions) and the psychological profile that enables developers to persist despite setbacks. The piece references Nathan Berman's MetroLoft, which is converting the former Pfizer Headquarters into residential rentals; the conversion recently suffered a partial collapse, highlighting execution risks in adaptive reuse. The author contrasts developer perseverance with regulatory and market caution, drawing on 40 years of observation.
stories.impact: Minimal actionable market intelligence. Pfizer HQ office-to-residential conversion in Manhattan is a trophy adaptive reuse project; the partial collapse signals construction and design risk in complex historic conversions. Developers should conduct thorough structural and MEP due diligence before committing to Midtown office-to-residential deals and ensure contingency budgets for unforeseen structural remediation. No systematic supply, demand, or capital market implications.
